A Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) model estimates what a company is worth by projecting its future free cash flows and then discounting those back to their present value. This method is particularly useful for companies like CRISPR Therapeutics, where long-term growth and future earnings are the main drivers of value.

A company’s growth expectations and risk profile can meaningfully shift what investors consider a “normal” or “fair” P/B ratio. High growth prospects or lower risk might warrant a higher P/B multiple. In contrast, slower growth or more uncertainty typically leads to a discount versus peers.

Disc Medicine currently trades at a price-to-book (P/B) ratio of 5.9x, using the last close price of $89.95. This level suggests investors are assigning a premium to Disc Medicine’s net asset value, compared both to the sector and to its closest peers.

The price-to-book ratio reflects what investors are willing to pay for each dollar of company assets. For biotech firms like Disc Medicine, this ratio can be especially significant because it incorporates market hopes for the company’s future scientific and commercial breakthroughs beyond its current book value.

At 5.9x, Disc Medicine’s P/B ratio is higher than the US Biotechs industry average of 2.5x.
